coredata.dc:title: Iron-carbon dots embedded in molybdenum single-atom nanoflowers as multifunctional nanozyme for dual-mode detection of hydrogen peroxide and uric acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jin
Lian, Tao
Liu, Sipei
Zhong, Jiali
Cheng, Rou
Tang, Xiaomin
Xu, Peng
Qiu, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have attracted extensive attention in the field of catalysis due to their excellent catalytic ability and enhanced atomic utilization, but the multi-mode single-atom nanozymes for biosensors remain a challenging issue. In this work, iron-doped carbon dots (Fe CDs) were loaded onto the edges and pores of Mo SACs with nanoflower morphology; accordingly, a composite material Fe CDs/Mo SACs was prepared successfully, which improves the catalytic performance and develops a fluorescence mode without changing the original morphology. The steady-state kinetic data indicates that the material prepared have better affinity for substrates and faster reaction rates under optimized conditions. The specific kinetic parameters Km and Vmax were calculated as 0.39 mM and 7.502×10−7 M·s−1 respectively. The excellent peroxidase-like activity of Fe CDs/Mo SACs allows H2O2 to decompose into •OH, which in turn oxidizes colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). At the same time, the fluorescence signal of Fe CDs/Mo SACs quenches obviously by DAP at 460 nm through internal filtration effect (IFE), while the characteristic fluorescence response of DAP gradually increases at 590 nm. Based on this sensing mechanism, a sensitive and accurate dual-mode (colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent) sensor was constructed to detect H2O2 and uric acid, and the rate of recovery and linearity were acceptable for the detection of UA in human serum and urine samples. This method provides a new strategy for rapid and sensitive detection of UA, and also broadens the development of SACs in the field of biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe CDs/Mo SACs
Nanozyme
Colorimetry
Ratiometric fluorescence
Uric acid

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: POD-like activities of Fe CDs/Mo SACs
Optical properties of Fe CDs/Mo SACs
Sensing mechanism of H2O2 and UA
Dual-mode detection of H2O2 and UA
Determination of UA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001). Compliance with ethical standards. The study was approved by the ethics committee of Jiangxi Medical College, and their guidelines were followed throughout this study. The serum samples involved in our research were from gout individuals. Informed consent was obtained from all human participants.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21765015 , 21808099 ), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province ( 20192BCD40001 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthetic process of Fe/Mo DSACs. (b, c) The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of Mo SACs and Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (d) The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (e) HRTEM image of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (f) EDS mapping images of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (g) Size distribution of Fe CDs/Mo SACs.

(a, b) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s and N 1 s in Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (c) FT-IR spectra of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (d, e) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of Mo SACs and Fe CDs/Mo SACs (inset: pore size distribution of Mo SACs and Fe CDs/Mo SACs). (f) Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) and derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) thermograms of Fe CDs/Mo SACs.

(a) Schematic illustration of POD-like activity of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD (b), TMB (c) and ABTS (d) oxidation catalyzed by Fe CDs/Mo SACs in the presence of different concentration of H2O2. (e, f) Steady-state kinetic assay of Fe CDs/Mo SACs and their responding double reciprocal diagram in the insert.

(a) Schematic diagram of the mechanism for detecting UA based on Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra for Fe CDs/Mo SACs with excitation wavelengths from 320 to 400 nm. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of DAP and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (d) The fluorescence lifetime spectrum of Fe CDs/Mo SACs with and without DAP. (e) The Zeta potentials of Fe CDs/Mo SACs and DAP. (f) Fluorescence spectrum and (g) UV–vis absorption spectrum of various combinations between Fe CDs/Mo SACs and different substances (inset: the image under excitation of ultraviolet light at365 nm and under sunlight).

(a, d) Fluorescence emission spectra at the excitation of 370 nm with different H2O2 (0.2–250 μM) and different UA (0.2–200 μM) (inset: the corresponding color evolutions upon ultraviolet light). (b, e) CIE color coordinates of the system of H2O2 + Fe CDs/Mo SACs and UA + Fe CDs/Mo SACs. (c, f) UV–vis spectroscopy with different H2O2 (0.2–300 μM) and different UA (0.2–150 μM). (g, h) Fluorescence and colorimetric selective detection UA (The red and blue columns represent the presence and absence of UA respectively), Each letter represent different substances: (1) ascorbic acid (2) cysteine (3) glutathione (4) dopamine (5) glucose (6) urea (7) glycine (8) NaCl (9) KCl (10) CaCl2 (11) NH4Cl and (12) UA.
Comparison of various methods for UA determination.
Fluorescence determination of UA in human serum and urine samples (n = 3).
Colorimetry determination of UA in human serum and urine samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Iron-carbon dots embedded in molybdenum single-atom nanoflowers as multifunctional nanozyme for dual-mode detection of hydrogen peroxide and uric acid

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China (P. Qiu).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have attracted extensive attention in the field of catalysis due to their excellent catalytic ability and enhanced atomic utilization, but the multi-mode single-atom nanozymes for biosensors remain a challenging issue. In this work, iron-doped carbon dots (Fe CDs) were loaded onto the edges and pores of Mo SACs with nanoflower morphology; accordingly, a composite material Fe CDs/Mo SACs was prepared successfully, which improves the catalytic performance and develops a fluorescence mode without changing the original morphology. The steady-state kinetic data indicates that the material prepared have better affinity for substrates and faster reaction rates under optimized conditions. The specific kinetic parameters Km and Vmax were calculated as 0.39 mM and 7.502×10−7 M·s−1 respectively. The excellent peroxidase-like activity of Fe CDs/Mo SACs allows H2O2 to decompose into •OH, which in turn oxidizes colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). At the same time, the fluorescence signal of Fe CDs/Mo SACs quenches obviously by DAP at 460 nm through internal filtration effect (IFE), while the characteristic fluorescence response of DAP gradually increases at 590 nm. Based on this sensing mechanism, a sensitive and accurate dual-mode (colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent) sensor was constructed to detect H2O2 and uric acid, and the rate of recovery and linearity were acceptable for the detection of UA in human serum and urine samples. This method provides a new strategy for rapid and sensitive detection of UA, and also broadens the development of SACs in the field of biosensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA), as one of the end products of purine metabolism in the human body, is closely related to the level of human health. In general, the normal value of UA in human serum is 120–450 μM and in urine is 1.4–4.4 mM [1]. The abnormal expression of UA in human body is highly correlated with a series of diseases including gout, diabetes, leukemia and cardiovascular diseases etc [2,3]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a rapid, sensitive and accurate method to monitor uric acid in the human body in real time to protect human health.

According to the literature reports, the current methods for detecting UA are blossoming like flowers in spring, including high-performance liquid chromatography [4], Raman spectroscopy [5], electrochemical method [6], fluorescence spectroscopy [7], and colorimetry [8]. Fluorescence spectrometry embraces the characteristics of high sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy. Ratiometric fluorescence spectroscopy is usually considered to measure the concentration of the analyte in the sample with the benefit of the ratio of the fluorescence intensities of two fluorescent substances, which not only avoids the influence of various external factors on the test results, but also significantly improves the accuracy and reliability compared to single-emission fluorescence [9]. Obviously, the colorimetric method has the advantages of visible color changes to the naked eye, simple equipment, and convenient operation. Therefore, the development of a multifunctional probe for ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode can integrate these merits to achieve the synthesis and the integration of the original techniques in visualized detection of UA.

Recent progress on the enzyme-mimic catalyst indicates that nanozymes often display enhanced properties including high stability, strong catalytic ability, and low cost compared with natural enzymes, which have a place in the fields of biosensing, disease treatment, environmental remediation and electrocatalysis [10–13]. Since the concept of SACs was proposed by Zhang's group in 2011 [14], SACs have been developed rapidly under extensive research due to their high atomic utilization rate, unique catalytic performance, and stability [15]. However, the lack of optical performance has limited their extensive and thorough application in certain fields. It is widely known that the presence of unoccupied d-orbitals of Mo results in a strong adsorption of N, which in turn stabilizes the formation of Mo-N covalent bonds. Therefore, Mo SACs have become an outperformer in electrocatalysis and biosensing due to their enhanced atomic utilization and catalytic performance [16,17]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted much attention for their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and green and simple preparation method [18]. Therefore, Mo SACs of high catalytic activity are recommended to go hand in hand with high fluorescence performance CDs, which not only compensates for the lack of fluorescence performance of SACs, but also improves certain catalytic performance, providing a greater platform for the development of SACs and laying a solid foundation for our fluorescence colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform.

The soft-template sacrificial method was designed to synthesize molybdenum single-atoms catalysts (Mo SACs). Pluronic F127 and ammonium molybdate were used as the soft template and molybdenum source, respectively, whereas dopamine was chosen as the carbon and nitrogen source, which undergoes a process of self-polymerization and high-temperature carbocation to form the end product with a uniformly distributed molybdenum single-atoms on the basis of nitrogen-doped carbon. The initial product of Fe CDs was synthesized with a simple hydrothermal method and purified through dialysis. Fe CDs was adsorbed on the surface of Mo SACs by electrostatic attraction to obtain the final product (Fe CDs/Mo SACs), which in subsequent tests were shown to have excellent peroxidase-like activity (POD-like activity) as well as certain fluorescence properties, providing a channel for dual-mode detection UA. Therefore, H2O2 generated by UA under the specific catalysis of uricase was decomposed by Fe CDs/Mo SACs to generate •OH, which oxidized the OPD to DAP, accompanied by the color of the system changing from colorless to yellow. At the same time, the fluorescence of the material at 460 nm was gradually quenched by DAP through IFE, while the characteristic fluorescence peak of DAP at 590 nm was gradually enhanced, and the I 590/I 460 showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of UA. In conclusion, the dual-read method (ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry) for the detection of UA was successfully constructed and then applied to fast quantify of UA in real samples with satisfactory results including human serum and urine.

The synthesis process of Fe CDs/Mo SACs was described in Fig. 1 a. The synthesis of Mo SACs was carried out using template sacrifice method according to the published procedure with some modifications [19]. Pluronic F127 used as a soft template and dopamine was selected as a carbon and nitrogen source, then dopamine undergoes self-polymerization and combines with ammonium molybdate to form block copolymers in alkaline medium. During the subsequent calcination process at 350℃ and 800℃ in turn, the template and unreacted raw materials were removed, leaving the product of nitrogen doped carbon as the base and uniformly distributed molybdenum single atoms. As seen in Fig. 1b and Fig. S1, the SEM images revealed that the Mo SACs exhibited a uniformly dispersed nanoflower structure with the hydrodynamic diameter of 690 nm. In addition, the most common hydrothermal method was used to prepare Fe CDs and the obtained Fe CDs was purified by simple dialysis. The high-resolution TEM images show the uniform spherical morphology of Fe CDs, and the diffraction peak at 24° in the XRD pattern corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon. These results confirm the successful synthesis of Fe CDs (Fig. S2). Fe CDs was bound to adsorb slowly onto the surface of Mo SACs under the condition of continuous stirring, which imparted fluorescent properties to the non-fluorescent Mo SACs (Fig. S3a-b). The Zeta potential was tested to investigate the reason for the binding of Mo SACs and Fe CDs. As shown in the Fig. S3c, the results showed that the potentials of Mo SACs, Fe CDs and Fe CDs/Mo SACs were −20.33 mV, 2.06 mV and −16.55 mV, respectively. The opposite potential between Mo SACs and Fe CDs provides conclusive evidence for electrostatic attraction. Furthermore, there was an interesting sequel that the microstructure and properties of Fe CDs/Mo SACs can be adjusted conveniently by controlling the doping ratio of Mo SACs and Fe CDs. The morphological changes of Fe CDs/Mo SACs at different ratios, 50:1, 10:1, 5:1, 1:1, 1:5, and 1:10, were monitored by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images. As shown in the Fig. S4a-b, when the ratio was 50:1 and 10:1 respectively, the material was uniformly dispersed and maintained the original nanoflower morphology. As the ratio decreased to 5:1 and 1:1, a large degree of aggregation occurred to form large clusters (Fig. S4c-d). However, when the ratio continues to decreases to 1:1 and 1:10, the material not only displayed greater aggregation, but disorderly stacking and structure collapses can be observed (Fig. S4e-f). These results demonstrate the importance of Fe CDs in determining the final morphology of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. The change of morphology was probably because CDs are prone to aggregation [20] and the honeycomb-like nanoflower tends to be split owing to the incorporation of CDs [21]. From the high-resolution SEM (Fig. S5), it can be clearly seen that Fe CDs are loaded on the surface and folds of the nanoflower, which grows a small bulge. As the content of Fe CDs increased, the thickness of the petal-like nanosheets became thicker, leading to the narrowing of the corresponding channels, and the bulges become more obvious, gradually causing the morphology of Mo SACs to change or even collapse. In addition, Fe CDs/Mo SACs with different doping ratios exhibited slightly diverse POD-like activity which was investigated by oxidizing colorless OPD to yellow DAP in the presence of H2O2. As shown in the Fig. S6a, the strongest catalytic ability to oxidize OPD was exhibited when the doping ratio of Mo SACs and Fe CDs was 10:1 under the same conditions, which could be attributed to the more effective active sites provided by the uniformly dispersed nanoflower-like nanozymes. It was worth noting that the catalytic ability of Fe CDs/Mo SACs was found to be stronger than that of Mo SACs and Fe CDs, proving that there was a synergistic effect between Mo SACs and Fe CDs. In addition, comparing the catalytic activity of Fe CDs and CDs without iron, the results showed that Fe CDs had a higher absorbance value at 450 nm than CDs, indicating the crucial role of iron in the catalytic process (Fig. S6b). To sum up, the materials with Mo SACs: Fe CDs = 10:1 was selected for subsequent experiments.

As shown in the Fig. 1d, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Fe CDs/Mo SACs exhibited the typical nanoflower structure, which was consistent with the SEM results (Fig. 1c). From high-resolution TEM images (Fig. 1e), it can be seen that there were ordered lattice fringes at the edges of the nanoflowers, with a spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon [22]. In addition, element mapping was used to analyze the elemental composition of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. The results showed that C, N, Mo, and Fe were uniformly distributed in Fe CDs/Mo SACs, and C, N, and Mo exhibited most colocalization, while Fe was only slightly distributed on the surface of the nanoflowers (Fig. 1f). The particle size distribution histogram results show that the hydrodynamic diameter of Fe CDs/Mo SACs was about 740 nm (Fig. 1g), slightly increasing compared to the diameter of Mo SACs (690 nm). This results indirectly proves the successful incorporation of Fe CDs into Mo SACs.

The elemental composition and content of Fe CDs/Mo SACs were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The appearance of weak characteristic peaks of Fe in the full spectrum indicate that Fe CDs were successfully loaded on the surface or in the mesopores of the nanoflowers (Fig. S7a). The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2 a) can be deconvoluted into three peaks belonging to CC (284.8 eV), CN (286.4 eV), and O-CO (288.9 eV), indicating that the surface of Fe CDs/Mo SACs is decorated with abundant CO, CO, and CN species [23,24]. The spectra of N 1 s (Fig. 2b) can be divided into 398.7 eV, 399.6 eV, 400.3 eV, and 402.1 eV, which can be assigned to pyrridic-N, Nx-Mo, pyrrolic-N, and oxidized-N [25–27]. Pyrridic-N and pyrrolic-N contribute to the formation of π conjugated systems for anchoring Mo sites. The peaks at 229.4, 233.1 and 236.3 eV correspond to Mo2+3d5/2, Mo6+3d5/2, and Mo6+3d3/2, respectively (Fig. S7b) [28]. As shown in Fig. S7c, the Fe 2p spectrum mainly exhibits two weak peaks of Fe2p1/2 and Fe2p3/2 at 711.5 and 725.4 eV [29]. X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) was also used to analyze the crystal structure of nanozymes. As shown in the Fig. S7d, the main peak at 25.2° corresponds to (002) of disordered graphite [30], and the subsequent weak peak coincides with the structure of FeN (PDF#50–1087), which is mainly due to the binding of iron ions with carbon in the Fe CDs or the cross-linking of Fe CDs with a nitrogen and carbon co-doped frame in the Mo SACs.

The functional groups of Mo SACs, Fe CDs, and Fe CDs/Mo SACs were explored through FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in the Fig. 2c, the bands at 3450 cm−1 and around 500–1000 cm−1 were related to υ (OH) and υ (N-Mo/N-Fe) [31]. For Fe CDs, the peaks at 1560 and 1650 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration absorption peak of CO, which also appear at the same positions as Fe CDs/Mo SACs. The peaks at 1120 and 1630 cm−1 correspond to the CO and CN of Mo SACs, and their positions were basically consistent with Fe CDs/Mo SACs [32]. It can be seen that Mo SACs and Fe CDs/Mo SACs conform to type-IV nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm from Fig. 2d-e, demonstrating that both of them possessed mesoporous structure [33]. As shown in the Table S1, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of Mo SACs was calculated to be 66.576 m2/g, with average pore size of 13.59 nm (Fig. 2d). After binding with Fe CDs, the specific surface area and average pore size decreased to 61.039 m2/g and 11.284 nm (Fig. 2e), indicating that Fe CDs have been successfully doped into the mesopores of Mo SACs. Compared with other mesoporous materials, the larger specific surface area and pore size of Fe CDs/Mo SACs were conducive to exposing more surface active sites, providing ample opportunities for electron transfer and catalysis [34]. In addition, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric derivative analysis (DTG) were used to research the characteristics and chemical structure of Fe CDs/Mo SACs during pyrolysis (Fig. 2f). The weight reduction rate was obtained from the DTG curve, and the thermal degradation content can be calculate using the peak values in the DTG curve. The first slight weight loss at around 60 °C may be attributed to the residual trace ethanol volatilization in the material. The second weight loss at around 210 °C corresponds to the collection of functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs. The 10 % weight loss between 260 to 320 °C was due to the decomposition of amide, ether bond, and hydroxyl group in the CDs. The final loss occurs in the range of 350 to 510 °C, which may correspond to the decomposition of ether bond and hydroxyl group in the CDs or the pyrolysis of certain cross-linked carbon networks [35,36]. In summary, there was no doubt that Fe CDs/Mo SACs was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD-like activities of Fe CDs/Mo SACs
ce:section.ce:para: The typical chromogenic substrate colorimetric test in the presence of H2O2 was carried out to investigate the POD-like activity of Fe CDs/Mo SACs (Fig. 3 a). Compared to the control group, the characteristic absorption peak at 450 nm was observed when Fe CDs/Mo SACs, OPD and H2O2 were present simultaneously, and the absorbance values increased gradually with the concentration of H2O2, accompanied with a gradual deepening in color of the system (Fig. 3b). The same results have also been achieved in the other two classic peroxidase substrates TMB and ABTS (Fig. 3c-d), which richly validated that Fe CDs/Mo SACs have excellent POD-like activity. Free radical capture and scavenging experiments were conducted to further reveal the mechanism of POD-like activity. According to literature reports, thiourea can be used as a scavenger for •OH in experiments. As shown in the Fig. S8, the color development and fluorescence emission of DAP to varying degrees could be displayed with different concentrations of thiourea, so •OH as the main active produced reactive oxygen species (ROS), was indispensable to realize efficient catalysis. Based on this classical mechanism, steady-state kinetic experiments were used to further explore the catalytic properties of Fe CDs/Mo SACs nanozymes. Specifically, the absorbance curve over time was obtained by keeping one substrate concentration constant and changing the concentration of another substrate. Km and Vmax , representing substrate affinity and maximum reaction rate, were important parameters used to determine the level of enzyme activity, which were obtained through analyzing and calculating the Michaelis-Menten curve and Lineweaver-Burk plot. From Fig. 3e-f, it is clear that when the H2O2 concentration remains constant, the two important parameters Km and Vmax were obtained for 0.72 mM and = 7.502 × 10-7 M·s−1, respectively. When the substrate was replaced with OPD, Km and Vmax were calculated to be 0.39 mM and 6.29 × 10-8 M·s−1, respectively. As a comparison, kinetic tests were also conducted on Mo SACs and Fe CDs (Fig. S9). The lower Km and higher Vmax values of Fe CDs/Mo SACs not only demonstrate their excellent catalytic ability, but also indicate a synergistic effect between Mo SACs and Fe CDs. In addition, as shown in the Table S2, in contrast to other nanozymes, Fe CDs/Mo SACs embrace the lower Km values indicating that Fe CDs/Mo SACs have good affinity for substrates [37–40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of Fe CDs/Mo SACs
ce:section.ce:para: It is necessary to further study the fluorescence properties of Fe CDs/Mo SACs. As shown in the Fig. 4 b, the fluorescence of Fe CDs/Mo SACs exhibits an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior at 320–400 nm, which was consistent with the characteristics of most carbon nanomaterials [41,42]. Moreover, when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, the strongest fluorescence emission intensity was observed at 460 nm. Fe CDs/Mo SACs aqueous solution appears black under sunlight, while bright blue fluorescence is visualized under ultraviolet light (Fig. S10a). As shown in the Fig. S10b, fluorescence intensities at 460 nm were not significant differences between Fe CDs and Fe CDs/Mo SACs, indicating that they have the same fluorescence group and also indicating the successful recombination of Fe CDs with Mo SACs. In addition, the photostability of Fe CDs/Mo SACs was investigated. The results showed that within 1000 s of irradiation, there was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of Fe CDs/Mo SACs, and the fluorescence signal could maintain about 90 % within 14 days (Fig. S10c-d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of H2O2 and UA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a shows the sensing mechanism of dual-mode method based on Fe CDs/Mo SACs for the detection of H2O2 and UA. Allantoin and H2O2 were produced after the specific catalysis of UA and uricase [43], then H2O2 was catalyzed by Fe CDs/Mo SACs to form •OH, which in turn oxidized OPD to DAP. At this time, the color of the system gradually changes from colorless to yellow as the concentration of UA increases, which was the theoretical basis of colorimetric assay for UA. On the other hand, the blue fluorescence of Fe CDs/Mo SACs at 460 nm under 370 nm excitation was quenched by DAP, and the characteristic yellow fluorescence of DAP was emitted at 590 nm. Therefore, the fluorescence assay for UA can be proposed with the aid of the ratio of fluorescence intensity I 590/I 460.

In order to investigate the mechanism of interaction between Fe CDs/Mo SACs and DAP, the fluorescence excitation, emission spectra, and UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe CDs/Mo SACs were studied. Fig. 4c shows that the emission spectrum of Fe CDs/Mo SACs at 460 nm overlaps significantly with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DAP at 450 nm, providing possibilities for internal filtering effect (IFE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [44,45]. Generally, fluorescence lifetimes and zeta potential are considered to be important means to distinguish IFE and FRET. As shown in the Fig. 4d, there was no significant difference in fluorescence lifetimes between the presence and absence of DAP in the system. In addition, the zeta potentials of Fe CDs/Mo SACs and DAP were −16.55 mV and −12.07 mV, respectively (Fig. 4e). The same negative charge resulted in electrostatic repulsion between the two substances, preventing the FRET effect from occurring. To further validate the IFE effect, we corrected the fluorescence intensity and UV–Vis absorbance using Equation (1), where F obsd is the observed maximum FL intensity and F cor is the corrected maximum FL intensity after removing IFE from F obsd. The relevant correction results are shown in the Fig. S11 and Table S3. Compared with E obsd and E cor, it was found that the observed quenching efficiency was significantly higher than the corrected quenching efficiency. Moreover, the IFE inhibition efficiency of DAP on the Fe CDs/Mo SACs was as high as 91 % of the total inhibition efficiency, indicating that the inhibition efficiency mainly came from IFE. As discussed above, we can safely come to the conclusion that the fluorescence of Fe CDs/Mo SACs at 460 nm was quenched by DAP through IFE.

The implementation of some controlled experiment was used to explore the feasibility of dual-mode biosensors for the detection of UA and H2O2. From the fluorescence spectrum shown in the Fig. 4f, the change of the fluorescence curve could almost be neglected when Fe CDs/Mo SACs were arbitrarily combined with other substances. It is worth mentioning that only when Fe CDs/Mo SACs, UA, Uricase and OPD coexisted in the system, the fluorescence peak at 460 nm was significantly quenched, and a new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 590 nm. Mean, the similar results were gained in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4g). In brief, when Fe CDs/Mo SACs, UA, Uricase and OPD were added to the system together, the absorbance value at 450 nm was significantly enhanced, indicating that a large amount of DAP was generated. There were no peaks in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the other groups, indicating that OPD was not oxidized, which suggested that Fe CDs/Mo SACs do not possess OXD-like activity, but exhibit excellent POD-like performance.

In order to improve the sensitivity of the sensor, it is necessary to comprehensively explore the factors related to the experiment. In previous reports, the process of specific incubation of UA and uricase to produce H2O2 was stable and mature enough, with an optimal pH of 8.5, temperature of 35℃, and incubation time of 15 min [46,47]. Based on the optimal conditions, the effects of pH, temperature, time, and the concentration of OPD on the ratiometric fluorescence (I 590/I 460) during the catalytic process were thoroughly investigated. As shown in the Fig. S12a, it was found that I 590/I 460 increased with the increase of pH, and reached the maximum at pH = 5. We explored the effect of temperature between 25 and 60℃ and found that as the temperature gradually increased, I 590/I 460 gradually increased and stabilized at 55℃ (Fig. S12b). According to the Fig. S12c, it is evident that when the time was 30 min and the OPD concentration was 6 mM, I 590/I 460 basically reached the platform (Fig. S12d). Therefore, the experiments have demonstrated that Fe CDs/Mo SACs exhibit the optical POD-like performance at pH = 5, 55℃, 30 min, and 6 mM OPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode detection of H2O2 and UA
ce:section.ce:para: A dual-mode biosensor was constructed using the optimal experimental conditions of appeal to achieve sensitive and accurate detection of H2O2 and UA. According to the sensing mechanism discussed earlier, there should be a good linear relationship between I 590/I 460 and UA concentration, and the absorbance of the solution could increase linearly with the increase of UA concentration.

As shown in the Fig. 5 a and S13a, I590/I 460 increases linearly with the increase of H2O2 in the range of 0.1–250 μM. The linear equation was I 590/I 460 = 0.00901CH2O2 + 0.19893 (R2 = 0.994), and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.056 μM. The significant fluorescence changes from blue (0.2183, 0.2267) to yellow (0.4610, 0.4151) was recorded through CIE (Commission Internationale de l′Eclairage) chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 5b). For the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the linear relationship was established between the absorbance at 450 nm and the concentration of H2O2 within the range of 0.1–300 μM. The linear equation was expressed for ΔA = 0.00602CH2O2 + 0.02445 (R2 = 0.995) with the LOD of 0.092 μM (Fig. 5c and S13b). It is worth explaining that ΔA = A-A0, where A and A0 represent the absorbance values of the solution in the presence and absence of the analyte, respectively.

In addition, the detection results of UA by biosensors indicated that I 590/I 460 showed a good linear correlation with UA concentration in the range of 0.1–200 μM. The linear fitting equation was I 590/I 460 = 0.01856CUA + 0.53602 (R2 = 0.993), and LOD was 0.03 μM (Fig. 5d and S13c). The CIE coordinates (Fig. 5e) indicate a gradual transition of fluorescence from blue (0.2745, 0.2699) to yellow (0.447, 0.4153). Fig. 5f and S13d shows the linear relationship between the absorbance value of DAP and the concentration of UA in the range of 0.2–150 μM. The calibration curve was ΔA = 0.00535CUA + 0.02446 (R2 = 0.991), and the LOD was estimated as 0.11 μM. Table 1 presents a comparison between our constructed sensor and other methods. This dual-mode method was comparable or even better than previous reports in terms of linear range and LOD for detecting UA [48–53], indicating that our method has better sensing performance.

Selectivity and anti-interference ability were important means to evaluate the practicality of the dual-mode sensors for detecting UA. In order to test the specificity of the sensor for UA, common interfering substances in serum and urine were selected for experimental analysis, such as macromolecular substances (ascorbic acid, cysteine, glutathione, dopamine, glucose, urea, glycine) and some inorganic salt ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, NH4 +). The concentration of UA during the experiment was controlled for 30 μM, ascorbic acid, glutathione, cysteine and dopamine were selected at a concentration of 10 μM because of their low content in human serum and urine [54,55]. In addition, the concentration of the other substances was ten times that of UA. The experimental results shown in the Fig. 5g indicate that when the interfering substances were added separately to the system, the values of I 590/I 460 and the absorbance value of 450 nm were remained basically unchanged, which proves that the biosensor based on Fe CDs/Mo SACs has good selectivity for UA. When the interfering substance coexists with UA in the system, neither the value of I 590/I 460 nor the absorbance value of 450 nm are affected (Fig. 5h). Therefore, we can conclude that our proposed dual-mode sensor can be applied to the sensitive and accurate detection of UA in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of UA in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we firstly synthesized Mo SACs with nanoflower morphology through soft-template sacrifice method, and the multifunctional Fe CDs/Mo SACs was prepared by combining Fe CDs with Mo SACs through a simple stirring way. Interestingly, SEM, TEM, BET, EDS, and FT-IR techniques have shown that Fe CDs are anchored at the edges and pores of nanoflowers. The doping of CDs not only synergistically enhanced the POD-like activity of Mo SACs, but also endowed Mo SACs with unique fluorescence characteristics. The dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescence quantitative analysis of H2O2 and UA was achieved based on the oxidation of OPD by •OH and the quenching of Fe CDs/Mo SACs fluorescence by DAP through IFE. Furthermore, it can be concluded that the dual-mode sensor has practicality and accuracy in human serum and urine samples. Collectively, the research provides a new method for enhancing the performance of SACs and will also promote the development and application of single-atom nanozymes in multimodal sensing processes.

Jin Chen: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Tao Lian: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation. Sipei Liu: Investigation, Visulaization. Jiali Zhong: Methodology, Investigation. Rou Cheng: Validation. Xiaomin Tang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Peng Xu: Supervision, Data curation. Ping Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001).

The study was approved by the ethics committee of Jiangxi Medical College, and their guidelines were followed throughout this study. The serum samples involved in our research were from gout individuals. Informed consent was obtained from all human participants.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.04.110.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
